hints.junior: Skeletons work when you know the shape of what's loading. Spinners work when you don't. A skeleton for a search result with unknown count is the wrong choice — use a spinner.
hints.mid: The 200ms rule applies to both: don't flash a skeleton or spinner for fast loads. Add a delay: only show the indicator if the load takes longer than 200ms.
hints.staff: Skeleton screens improve perceived performance by giving the brain a prediction to confirm. But they fail when the layout doesn't match the skeleton — mismatched skeletons are worse than spinners because they create wrong expectations.

hints.junior: Toggle between the same content loading with a spinner vs a skeleton. Notice how the skeleton feels faster even when both have the same 1.2s load time.
hints.mid: The skeleton version eliminates the 'blank void' phase — the brain sees structure and starts processing. The spinner version is pure waiting. This is why skeleton improves perceived performance without changing real performance.
hints.staff: The effect is strongest for content-heavy layouts (feeds, cards, tables). For single-element loads (a modal, a button state), a spinner is more honest — no structure to predict. Match the indicator to the loading shape.

hints.junior: The concept chapter is the principle — read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
hints.mid: Connect the principle to your component API — what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
hints.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

hints.junior: Skim the code patterns here — you can copy the structure even if the syntax is new.
hints.mid: Compare broken vs fixed implementation — the diff is usually smaller than you expect.
hints.staff: Check edge cases: SSR, hydration, design tokens, and high-contrast mode.

anecdotes.junior: Facebook popularized skeleton screens in 2013 for the News Feed. Before: spinner while feed loaded. After: gray boxes in card shapes. Perceived load time dropped significantly with zero change to actual load time.
anecdotes.mid: The team I worked with applied skeleton screens to a dashboard — but the API sometimes returned 0, 1, or 10 items. The skeleton always showed 4 cards. When 0 cards loaded, the layout shift was jarring. We switched to a spinner for that endpoint.
anecdotes.staff: Lukew Wroblewski's research on skeleton screens distinguishes 'known-shape content' (profile, article, feed row) from 'unknown-shape content' (search results, dynamic lists). The rule: use skeletons only for known shapes, spinners for unknown counts.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're figuring out when to use skeleton screens vs spinners — and why the wrong choice makes loading feel worse.
sections.mid: This case covers the skeleton vs spinner decision matrix, the 200ms threshold rule, and how the 'predicted shape' is what makes skeletons work.
sections.staff: We're covering the psychology of predictive loading, the known-shape vs unknown-shape heuristic, and the failure modes of mismatched skeletons.
